coredata.dc:title: Microalgae biodiesel: A sustainable source of energy, unit operations, technological challenges, and solutions

coredata.dc:description: The decline in reserves of fossil fuels has shifted the world towards renewable and sustainable alternative fuels. Microalgae, a rich source of triacylglycerol, are a potential candidate for biodiesel production. The rising industrial uses of algae led to the development of different unit operations. Unraveling the biochemistry of compounds present in algae to produce biofuels and byproducts, biomass productivity and efficient extraction of bioactive compounds remain key hurdles. This review brings into light the different cell disruption methodologies, with reduced environmental hazards caused by conventional methods. The production of lipids for biofuel like biodiesel cannot alone sustain the algae-based economy. Therefore, using lipid extracted biomass for various other purposes serves it a solution. Lipid Extracted Biomass has numerous applications, including the production of biofuels like biohydrogen and biogas, biochar, bio-composites, carbon quantum dots and bio-polythene of different densities. It can also be used as animal feed, manure to improve soil fertility and to treat wastewater. The present article discusses the state-of-the-art technologies developed for algal biomass cultivation, harvesting and drying. This comparative discussion on different algal biofuels’ unit operations will reveal associated technological challenges arising during various unit operations, from cultivation to lipid extraction and its conversion to biodiesel. This study presents a sustainable solution for extracting lipids from microalgae by utilizing its byproducts for several commercial purposes. The study concludes that modifications are required in the conventional approaches of harvesting, drying along with cultivation to improve the bioeconomic potential of algae

row.entry: Organic solvents used include n-hexane, ethanol, methanol, chloroform, petroleum ether, dichloromethane, isopropanol etc.
Effective for small scale extraction, simple with high efficiency and economical due to less investment cost initially.
Time consuming, laborious, requires solvent in large volume which are highly flammable, toxic and their recovery is energy intensive.

Nonpolar/Organic solvents are used for cell lysis. In the extraction of lipids, such solvents interrupt the hydrophobic interactions which disrupts the linkage between non-polar and neutral lipids so as to obtain lipids.
Requires organic salts which melt at temperature lower than 100°C. It consists of small anion coupled with large organic cations.
Thermally stable, nonvolatile, recyclable, nonflammable and can be better substitute to conventional organic solvents.
At industrial-scale, use of such solvents will raise cost, not-ecofriendly as solvents are toxic, resist to thermal degradation and may hamper recovery of lipids.
Environmental costs can be reduced by using protic ionic compounds particularly [HNEt3] [H2SO4]. It reduces waste byproducts, loss of solvents and evolution of CO2.
Optimum pressure is necessary to maintain effectiveness. High pressure may cause overheating and blockage.
No solvent required, operational conditions are less, easy to operate, requires less maintenance
Slow, requires biomass in large quantity and less economical.
Cell disruption is caused by mechanical press which allows the release of lipids from dry biomass. It yields approximately 75% oil from biomass.
Temperature, type and concentration of solvent used, solid–liquid ratio time and dielectric properties of the processing mixture should be taken into consideration.
Safe, less amount of solvent used, eco-friendly, enhanced lipid yield, less operating cost and reaction time with reduced extraction time and cost.

Maintenance cost is viewed as a limiting factor at commercial scale. Centrifugation/ filtration becomes essential for the removal of solid residue. If the target compound or solvents used are non-polar, or volatile in nature, efficiency is reduced.

In the presence of microwaves, rapidly oscillating electric field get induced. Due to the interaction of this electric field with dielectric/polar material (water) heat is generated. This leads to formation of water vapors which eventually disrupt cells through electroporation.
Use of MAE with other mechanical methods like sonication improves the productivity of lipids.

Parameters like ultrasonication power should be optimized as continued exposure can produce free radicals which may obstruct the quality of lipids due to oxidation. Moisture content and extraction time are to be maintained. Temperature should be kept low to prevent denaturation of biomolecules as heat get generated during processing.

Low operational costs, fast extraction time minimize the issues associated with conventional method of cell lysis, purity of end products is good due to better penetration of solvents in the cell and safe to the environment.

Difficult to scale up, power consumption is high, production of non-volatile pollutants due to reaction of hydroxyl ions and likely to react with biomolecules which can disrupt microalgal cells through cavitation bubbles.

Ultrasonic waves create microbubbles due to high-low pressure cycles around the cells which disrupt cells through cavitation. Small vacuum bubbles are produced during low pressure cycles which are collapsed by high pressure cycles. The rupture of these microbubbles generates shock waves that breaks the cell wall and cause the release of intracellular contents.

Higher lipid yield can be achieved by incorporating mixture having polar and non-polar solvents and increasing exposure time. The use of chemicals like cysteine, dithiothreitol or other compounds with solvents and flushing of solution with nitrogen can reduce the damage caused by free radicals/ions.
Temperature and nature of catalyst used (alkali, potassium hydroxide and sodium carbonate and the organic acids, acetic acid and formic acid) affects the quality and quantity of biocrude produced.
Ease of applicability both on wet (water content up to 80-95%) and dry algal biomass, efficient lipid recovery
High equipment and maintenance cost, upscaling is difficult. The emission of NOX in the processing due to nitrogen present in chlorophyll and proteins of algal cells is the major hurdle here.
Disruption of cells in the algal biomass is caused due to high pressure (10–25 MPa) and subcritical temperature (below 374°C).
Catalyst increases efficiency which results in high biocrude yield due to the incorporation of organic acids at higher temperature.
Easy operability can be hampered by cell wall properties of algal cells
Easy to upscale and low equipment cost
Not applied widely

Concentration/movement of water in the algal cell membrane get discontinue due to osmotic shock. The high concentration of solutes or other additives such as salt, substrates, neutral polymers (polyethylene glycol, dextran) cause rapid change in movement of cells which results in disruption of cells.
Methods like microwave extraction and sonication can enhance lipid recovery when used with this operation of cell disruption
Cell wall properties of an oleaginous microorganism is the essential factor
Easy to scale up, good lipid yield, minimum damage to target compound.
Procedural cost is high, not efficient as sulfide bonds present in the cell wall provide strength to the organelle.
Proteins like Cellulase or lysozyme disrupt the cell wall by digesting it and allow release of bioactive compounds such as lipids.
Incorporation of this with sonication may reduce cost and use of enzymatic hydrolysis with immobilized enzymes such as snailase,
Rigidity and strength of cell wall is the major consideration
Environmental cost is less as free nitrous acid (FNA) is a green and renewable chemical. Easy to upscale and production is also low.
Not employed widely.
Different concentrations of FNA are used as pre-treatment step with this for higher lipid yields.
Electric field applied is dependent on porosity of cell membrane. Pore formation is proportional to number and size of pore rather than surface area of cell wall.
Provides effective lipid production in terms of composition as well as quality. Efficient with respect to solvent used and time required in processing.
High energy cost and lipid yield is less
Application of electric field across the cell membrane generates aqueous pores and through enhanced mass transfer it results in release of lipid compounds from the cell.
Collaborated use of Ultrasound and High-pressure homogenization (HPH) for cell disruption would enhance lipid productivity.
Operating factors such as temperature, pressure and fluid flow rate are helpful to reduce energy cost and enhance lipid yield.
Less toxic, save time and remove the need of using conventional organic solvents by replacing them with solvent like CO2 gas.
High cost due to energy demand, operation and infrastructure necessity.

Substances having properties of both gases and liquids under conditions of high temperature and pressure are used. Due to this property fluids acts as an extracting solvent so as to perform extraction of lipids from algal cells without leaving much residue.
Fluids with properties such as intermediate viscosity or diffusion can be used which favors mass transfer equilibrium and enhance lipid productivity.
Energy consumption depends upon rigidity of cell wall, cell concentration, TAG content and HPH pressure
Useful for industries as it yields high value proteins, simple and easy to scale up.
High energy consumption, emulsion problems, extra-demulsification need to be performed for better oil extraction
HPH/homogenization facilitates cavitation across the algal cell membrane and dispersed phase of lipid in the slurry is obtained along with proteins and polysaccharides.
The combination of acid, alkali, and enzymes with this treatment provides improved lipid efficiency.
Effective cell disruption is dependent on various parameters such as bead size, bead-to-substrate ratio, concentration of biomass, slurry flow rate, speed and geometry of agitator.
Useful for large scale applications, high rate of cell disruption, yields high value compounds.
High energy consumption for heat generation and cooling, not economical.

It is equipped with vertical/horizontal cylindrical compartment with central shaft in combination of discs or other agitating elements. The compartment having beads and algal slurry of solvents with a spinning speed disrupt the cells.

Beads of Zirconium oxide (ZrO2) provide effective cell disruption due to its high specific density than glass beads. The speed of agitator and biomass concentration can be taken into consideration to increase kinetic rate constants.
High dependence on biomass concentration and selection of alkali and acids
Simple, low energy consumption, can be performed at pilot scale, efficient lipid productivity due to effective rupturing of cell walls
Not cost effective as neutralization of acids and alkalis can raise its cost and acids/ alkali can corrode equipment surfaces.
Alkali cleaves the fatty acyl chains from backbone moieties having phospholipids in the cell membrane and forms soap which is converted into fatty acids by acid neutralization.

Synergy of mechanical methods with this can reduce chemical requirement. Combined Algal Processing/CAP can be used where pretreated algal slurry transferred into fermenter to produce ethanol and lipid from it can be extracted by using hexane.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The decline in reserves of fossil fuels has shifted the world towards renewable and sustainable alternative fuels. Microalgae, a rich source of triacylglycerol, are a potential candidate for biodiesel production. The rising industrial uses of algae led to the development of different unit operations. Unraveling the biochemistry of compounds present in algae to produce biofuels and byproducts, biomass productivity and efficient extraction of bioactive compounds remain key hurdles. This review brings into light the different cell disruption methodologies, with reduced environmental hazards caused by conventional methods. The production of lipids for biofuel like biodiesel cannot alone sustain the algae-based economy. Therefore, using lipid extracted biomass for various other purposes serves it a solution. Lipid Extracted Biomass has numerous applications, including the production of biofuels like biohydrogen and biogas, biochar, bio-composites, carbon quantum dots and bio-polythene of different densities. It can also be used as animal feed, manure to improve soil fertility and to treat wastewater. The present article discusses the state-of-the-art technologies developed for algal biomass cultivation, harvesting and drying. This comparative discussion on different algal biofuels’ unit operations will reveal associated technological challenges arising during various unit operations, from cultivation to lipid extraction and its conversion to biodiesel. This study presents a sustainable solution for extracting lipids from microalgae by utilizing its byproducts for several commercial purposes. The study concludes that modifications are required in the conventional approaches of harvesting, drying along with cultivation to improve the bioeconomic potential of algae

ce:section.ce:para: Overexploitation of fossil fuels due to industrialization and population growth reflects a future with global energy crisis. While many sources to generate energy are being explored; some of them include hydro, tidal, solar, thermal and nuclear power. About 85% of our energy demand is facilitated by fossil fuels which are non-renewable in nature and cause adverse effects on our environment [Kumar, Rao, and Arumugam, 2015]. India being one of the top three crude oil importing countries, should pragmatically look for various other sources of energy fuels [Kumar and Bharadvaja, 2020]. Climate change induced by anthropogenic factors drive the global step towards microalgae based renewable energy resources/biofuels and nutritional food [Khan, Shin, and Kim, 2018]. Food crops like sugarcane, palm oil, jatropha, soyabean etc., have been used for the production of biofuels contributing to various generations of biofuel production [Kumar, Ghosh, and Pal, 2020] [Mondal et al., 2017]. However, biofuels extracted from different food crops or vegetables are not enough to meet the demands. While the production of biofuels from algae captures CO2 thus helping mitigate the global warming along with energy crisis [Brennan and Owende, 2010]. The lipid productivity yield availed by microalgae is higher than some of the best oil producing crops [Chisti, 2008] [Brennan and Owende, 2010]. Microalgae is proved to be rich source of various carbon compounds, used as nutraceuticals/health supplements, biofuels, wastewater remediation, feedstock for animals, manure for agricultural fields and bionanomaterials [Das, Aziz, and Obbard, 2011]. The use of microalgae as a sustainable resource for biofuels has opened new avenues for the production of biodiesel and judicious use of lipid extracted biomass to produce bioplastics, supercapacitor, human health products and substantially more [Hannon et al., 2010] [Troschl, 2018] [Matsagar et al., 2021].

Bioactive compounds extracted from microalgae comprises of lipids (Docosahexaenoic acid /DHA, polyunsaturated fatty acids (PUFAs), eicosapentaenoic acid/EPA), β-carotene, chlorophyll, polysaccharides, and proteins etc [Wells et al., 2017] [Kumar et al., 2021]. Lipids are biomolecules which can be polar or non-polar in nature. Polar lipids (phospholipids and glycolipids) are used in the formation of cell membranes while non polar lipids (acyl- glycerol and free fatty acids) serve as a source of energy [Subashchandrabose et al., 2013] [Subhash et al., 2017]. Fatty acids are the major source of lipids in microalgae, containing 12 to 22 carbons and their presence determine the quality and yield of biofuels [D'Alessandro and Antoniosi Filho, 2016]. TAG (triacylglycerol) is the main constituent responsible for biodiesel production due to its lower degree of saturation as compared to other lipid constituents [Halim, Danquah, and Webley, 2012]. Hence algae with high amount of unsaturated lipid content are considered potential candidates for biodiesel production. The field of algal research is now focusing on enhancing the accumulation of intracellular bioactive compounds, particularly lipids in order to combat energy problems.

Industries based on algae largely require biomass produced under various conditions like natural, controlled and semi-controlled environment. There are other considerations to enhance biomass productivity like cost-effectiveness, culture conditions and mode of cultivation. There are four major cultivation conditions which are widely used, such as phototrophic cultivation, heterotrophic cultivation, photoheterotrophic cultivation and mixotrophic cultivation [Kumar and Bharadvaja, 2021]. The process of lipid extraction before cell disruption involves harvesting of algal biomass mostly followed by drying. In case of wet biomass, the presence of water hampers the mass transfer of lipids which can lower the lipid yield [Chen, Li, and Wang, 2019] [Chen, Chang, and Lee, 2015]. The methods available for harvesting biomass include centrifugation, magnetic separation, filtration, floatation, flocculation and gravity sedimentation. These methods are based on different principles which can be applied as per the requirement of target compounds. Some are economical and others are environment friendly, while a combination of them has been reported to enhance recovery rate of biomass and lipids. Various methods of drying for algae are available such as solar/natural drying, cross-flow air drying, etc [Araujo et al., 2013]. Dry biomass gives lipid content varying from 20% to 50% of dry weight, and can reach up to 90% in some instances [Mata, Martins, and Caetano, 2010]. The step of drying may increase the cost of production. Natural solar drying damages the quality of target products and can also cause contamination. Thus, various treatment parameters necessarily affect the lipid content, such as method of biomass drying and harvesting, culturing media composition, its temperature, pH, light intensity and so on [Chokshi et al., 2016].

The most practiced procedure for lipid extraction utilizing Bligh & Dyer [Bligh and Dyer, 1959] and Folch [Folch, Lees, and Sloane Stanley, 1957] methods include the use of organic solvents. These organic solvents cause ecotoxicity. Hence, the dependence on such solvents for lipid extraction must be avoided. Thus, solvent free (minimum use of solvents) extraction techniques are needed for cell lysis. This can include osmotic shock, supercritical carbon dioxide (SCCO2) treatment and so on. While there are some other considerations that have effects on lipid productivity such as polarity of solvents used, moisture content of cell, cell structure and composition [Sati et al., 2019]. Microwave-assisted extractions, oil expeller, ultrasound-assisted extraction (UAE) and bead milling etc., are some of the pre-treatment techniques employed for efficient production of lipid from microalgae. These methods in conjunction with traditional approaches help in sustainable extraction of lipids.

This review article presents comprehensive discussion on different techniques which can be employed at different stages of lipid extraction [Mondal et al., 2017]. The study reveals that modifications are required in the conventional approaches of harvesting, drying along with cultivation in order to focus on high lipid productivity. Here different pre-treatment techniques are discussed by comparing the economic and environmental costs of each. It helps to find the sustainable solutions for depleting energy resources by developing green methods of lipid extraction and utilizing the lipid extracted biomass (LEB) to produce value-added products. Fig. 1

Microalgae being autotrophic in nature, have evolved their metabolic pathways so as to grow in different climatic conditions. The metabolism of reserving materials and energy takes place in the pre-steady growth or division stage of the algal cells. It is only due to nutrients rich culture and adequate growth conditions that the cell division in algae takes place [Das, Aziz, and Obbard, 2011].While culturing microalgae, metabolism of carbon plays a vital role in enhancing the performance of microalgae at industrial scale. The concentration of carbon dioxide in culture media affects the growth and productivity of algae. Generally, 2-5% of CO2 levels induce positive effects on photosynthesis and enhanced growth of algal cells. However, high and low levels (0.03% CO2, v/v) of CO2 generates negative impacts on growth [Cerri et al., 2017] [Sun et al., 2018].

Despite microalgae acting as natural carbon sink and encompassing potential to use larger environmental CO2 as compared to higher plants, their growth gets inhibited depending upon various factors. These include light harvesting, energy conversation and CO2 fixation [Koyande et al., 2019]. Thus, facilitating light source, be it natural or artificial, adds a growth enhancing or inhibiting aspect to the microalgal culture. Organic substrates upon oxidization accelerate the respiration rates and growth of the algal species [Fu et al., 2017]. Glucose, sucrose, fructose, glycerol and acetate are some of the carbon sources frequently used to increase rate of growth. The production of high amount of energy per mole in comparison to other sources makes glucose a frequently used carbon source in culture media [Melis, 2009]. Phototropic cultivation occurs in general, while heterotrophic method is aerobic in nature where energy is generated through assimilation of organic substrates by oxidative phosphorylation. In case of mixotrophic cultivation, which takes place in the presence of light, both inorganic CO2 and organic carbon sources are utilized [Wang, Yang, and Wang, 2014]. Different types of cultivation methods are summarized in table 1
.

A recent artificially developed photosynthesis system engaging hybrid inorganic-biological system could furnish the required chemical energy to the algal culture. It has proved to enhance the biomass-to-energy conversion by four folds. This has also reduced the solar-footprint requirement for culturing algal strains [Hann et al., 2022].

Cultivation of microalgae is the initial step in the production of biomass required to obtain bioactive compounds and other valuable products. In lipid production, harvesting the cultivated biomass plays an important role which has been discussed in the following section.

Upstream processing includes strain selection, growth and harvesting of algae while downstream has major role in extraction of valuable bioactive compounds [Araujo et al., 2013] [Gonçalves et al., 2015]. An effective dewatering technique for harvesting should produce algal biomass with 65–80 wt.% water [Dong et al., 2016]. In microalgae (size≤30µm), the presence of negative charge on the surface prevents them from settling under the influence of gravity. Therefore, harvesting techniques depends upon characteristics of algae including size, density and economical value of desired products [Singh and Patidar, 2018]. Whereas, an optimal method to harvest algae considers factors like selective extraction of needed intracellular materials, selection of strain, effect of harvesting methodand less use of chemicals and energy [Musa et al., 2019].

It is essential to focus that the selected technique should attain cell separation to its full potential with efficient recycling of water and nutrients in the extraction process at minimum cost. The processes of centrifugation facilitate efficient recovery of biomass at high cost while filtration works effectively for large algal cells but membrane clogging and need of surfactants add to the operational cost. Magnetic separation method for harvesting requires magnetically modified media, while flocculation is concerned more towards chemical contamination [Rosch, Skarka, and Patyk, 2009]. Some of the algal biomass harvesting techniques are discussed below in table 2
.

The use of different harvesting methods in conjunction can minimize the weakness of one process with the strength of another, yielding synergistic effects. Microalgal technology often requires different considerations for harvesting such as extraction of high yield value added products, non-interference of techniques with final products and reproducibility so as to get biomass for disruption of cells followed by drying.

In the extraction process of lipids from microalgae, dewatering process is carried out after harvesting of biomass [Christenson and Sims, 2011]. Drying is a process of removing moisture content resulting in inhibited water activity and microbial growth to preserve the biomass [Fellows, 2009]. The removal of water occurs in two steps, microalgal biomass removal from the culture media proceeded by elimination of water from biomass slurry [Harris et al., 2018]. However, drying is an energy intensive process but it enhances the efficiency of lipid extraction. The drying processes are applied to enable ease of storage, transportation and maximum use of compounds for biorefinery and later for food, fodder etc. There are a variety of factors to be considered for the selection of an effective method of drying such as energy efficiency, installation and procedural costs. Degradation of nutritional component, change in color, moisture content and drying kinetics are other major considerations for drying processes [Stramarkou et al., 2017]. Many potential technologies are available to dry microalgal biomass or any biomass having high viscosity [Prasetyaningrum and Djaeni, 2012] [Show, Lee, and Chang, 2013]. Solar drying is most frequently used natural drying process but has chances of contamination if performed in an open environment. Freeze drying however yields more retention of functional compounds which reflects towards adequate storage conditions for biomass [Larrosa et al., 2016]. Here we will discuss drying techniques like solar drying, rotary or drum drying, freeze drying and convective (Oven) drying in table 3
.

The drying procedure presents variable high or low rates of dryness, on the basis of selected method and moisture content. This influences the velocity of heat and mass transfer in samples, while non-diffusiveness occurs between outer and inner surfaces of biomass [Larrosa et al., 2016]. It is necessary to delve into more studies to improve the drying methods considering sensitivity towards heat, degradation phenomena and oxygen in microalgal biomass in order to preserve desired bioactive compounds.

The extraction of lipids can be performed both on wet and dry algal biomass. The traditional methods such as Bligh & Dyer and Folch were effective processes performed to quantitatively isolate bioactive compounds from either animal or plant cells, the recovery of extraction solvents and their environmental impacts remain a concern. This leads to introduction of some pre-treatment techniques which can be implemented with enhanced lipid recovery. The cell disruption methods performed as a part of pre-treatment are capable of reducing adverse environmental impacts and have proved to be economical in extraction processes by lowering the solvent requirements [Patel, Mikes, and Matsakas, 2018]. Disruption of cells can certainly enable efficient extraction of microalgal biomolecules when supplemented with different cultivation and harvesting methods as discussed earlier [Kim et al., 2013] [Lee, Lewis, and Ashman, 2012]. Some of the cell disruption methods need abundant amount of energy like ultrasound technique, while others are not economical like microwave-assisted and ionic methods as these are difficult in upscaling [Islam, Aryasomayajula, and Selvaganapathy, 2017]. On the basis of rigidity/flexibility of cell wall and the type of product required, various methods of cell disruption or pre-treatment are available; like solvent extraction, oil expeller, sonication etc. for efficient lipid extraction [Barba, Grimi, and Vorobiev, 2015].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conventional approach for total lipid extraction

ce:section.ce:para: The Bligh & Dyer method is considered as the standard method for total lipid extraction. It requires multiple steps which are used extensively in modified or original protocol form. The method was developed by using chloroform, methanol and water in a definite proportion (chloroform: methanol: water in ratio of 2:1:0.8 v/v respectively) to extract lipids [Bligh and Dyer, 1959]. A biphasic system is produced here due to presence of water. Chloroform contains lipid while methanol-water layer does not show its presence. Here modifications are done at cell lysis stage in order to get desired bio compounds [Zhang et al., 2018]. This method is widely used for both animal and plant cells, still having some disadvantages such as ecotoxicity due to organic solvents and tedious steps during processing which is unsuitable for large amounts of biomass.

This method was developed using a mixture of chloroform and methanol in the ratio of 2:1 [Folch, Lees, and Sloane Stanley, 1957]. The folch method produces a biphasic system where chloroform layer holds the lipid component and methanol possesses non-lipid compounds. This method is also used in modified form like Bligh & Dyer method at cell disruption stage. In some studies sonication is performed on dry biomass using chloroform: methanol (2:1) [Cheirsilp and Kitcha, 2015], followed by centrifugation [Chandra and Amit, 2019], homogenization etc. The variation in method of cell lysis will yield different co-product with the target compound.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges to conventional lipid extraction methods from microalgae

ce:list-item.ce:para: Moisture content in cell: It prevents the efficient contact between microalgal cells and extraction solvents. While the primary dewatering and subsequently performed optional drying processes remove water from the cells, however it may increase the overall operational cost. Considering the solvent-based lipid extraction method for wet biomass, water serves as a barrier between intracellular lipids and non-polar organic solvents [Chakraborty et al., 2016]. This hindrance can be solved by enhancing polarity of the solvent.

Cell structure and composition: The cell wall of algal cell is dynamic, complex and affects the interaction of the cell components with external environment as biological and biomechanical stabilities of the cell are controlled by it. Among different types of cells, the properties of cell wall play a crucial role in compositional heterogeneity. The efficiency of lipid extraction is generally hampered by the properties of cell membrane developed during its different growth phases [Reddy et al., 2014]. Thus, an effective method of cell disruption is required using different pre-treatment methods which are discussed in proceeding section.

Polarity of solvents: In the extraction of intracellular products like lipids, polarity of compound helps in the selection of suitable solvents. A mixture of polar-nonpolar solvents may improve lipid recovery. In this combination, the polar solvents would be responsible for the release of lipids from protein–lipid complexes which are soluble in non-polar solvents. The increase in polarity of solvents helps in the penetration of solvent into algal cells particularly when moisture is present in the cells. Solvents are the vital parameters to be considered during extraction and recovery of lipids from microalgal cells [Yoo et al., 2012].

Solvent free extraction techniques: The release of solvents into the environment affects not only the ecosystem but also makes it less economical. Thus, environmental issues and safety become a hurdle in organic solvent-based techniques. Hence, a solvent free extraction technique like supercritical CO2 method or use of ecofriendly solvents is recommended. There are various pre-treatment methods like microwave assisted, homogenization, bead milling etc. which are discussed in the proceeding section to solve the problem of using traditional solvents [Roux, Lamotte, and Achard, 2017].

ce:section.ce:para: The recovery of lipids and its purification encompasses high cost which becomes a hurdle in biofuel production. This requires developing such extraction methods/techniques that are cost-effective, safe and efficient, free of contamination, eco-friendly and can be executed at a large scale.

The performance of conventional extraction methods at a very large scale is not economical and environmentally viable. The solvent loss due to evaporation and remaining solvent after purification procedures makes us liable for environmental harm, adding high costs to the end products [Bharadvaja and Kumar, 2020]. In major instances, organic solvents or their co-solvents used are added with chlorinated solvents so as to potentially obtain maximum lipid yields [Khan, Shin, and Kim, 2018] [Chen et al., 2018]. However, the halogenated organic compounds are responsible for major harms such as carcinogenicity, ozone depletion, ground water contamination and other health hazards which compels the introduction of different pre-treatment techniques [Quinn and Davis, 2014].

There are different factors responsible for the efficient lipid recovery such as energy consumption, type and dosage of solvent, biomass concentration and status (wet/dried), toxicity and scalability etc. A number of pre-treatment methods are available like bead milling, electroporation, homogenization and osmotic shock to minimize the challenges associated with traditional approach [Kanda et al., 2012] [Saga et al., 2015]. Different unit operations involved during lipid recovery and techniques available for cell disruption (pre-treatment) are shown in Fig. 2
.

The biomass extracted after biofuel extraction can be used as cattle feed, manure in fields, feed for phyco- and aqua-culture as well as in the formation of carbon quantum dots [Plácido et al., 2019] [Bhardwaj and Bharadvaja, 2021]. There are numerous methods of cell lysis as shown in Fig. 2. The ultra sound assisted extraction (UAE) method of cell lysis is environment friendly but energy intensive therefore being unmanageable for up-scaling. While bead milling is a solvent free method, it is unable to provide high efficiency for rigid cell walled species. Enzymatic treatment is a particular enzyme-substrate interaction which requires long processing time with high capital cost [Patel, Mikes, and Matsakas, 2018]. A comparative study of various techniques or methods used for pre-treatment is summarized in the table 4
.

The above discussion reveals that the mechanical energy required for cell disruption is mostly used to produce heat. Thus, the process of disruption requires energy efficient techniques and chemical or biological method for sustainable extraction of lipids or other bioactive compounds [Barba, Grimi, and Vorobiev, 2015]. However, a combination of different cell disruption techniques has reported to enhance lipid yield along with environmental sustainability, energy savings and economic advantages. In some reports it was investigated that in biofuel production costs, 50-60% accounts for downstream processing [Harris et al., 2018]. The higher lipid productivity necessitates simplification of extraction processes of microalgal lipids and improvement in disruption as well extraction technologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of LEB (Lipid Extracted Biomass) for sustainable and economical algae-based biodiesel production
ce:section.ce:para:
The high operating costs of microalgal oil and perpetuating demand for fuel oil makes algae as a challenging option for biofuels [Christiansen et al., 2018]. In lipid extraction, the variation in techniques at various stages such as drying, harvesting and cell disruption increases the capital cost. Lipid extracted biomass (LEB) is approximately 30-75 % of the total dried biomass used for lipids extraction, depending upon processes applied and variety of species used during extraction [Ansari et al., 2017] [Gebremariam and Marchetti, 2018]. Therefore, economic competitiveness can be balanced by utilizing lipid extracted algal biomass for various other applications as shown in Fig. 3
.

Polyethylene is conventionally a product of petrochemical feedstocks. Such products are used in our day to day life but causing exploitation of fossil fuels and harming our ecosystem [Laurens et al., 2017]. Hence bioplastics are emerging necessity to replace plastic market. Plant-based polyethylene (Bio-PE) uses bioethanol as a source for its production [Zeller et al., 2013]. Bio-PE are produced by dehydrating bioethanol to form ethylene which is purified and polymerized to convert into Bio-polyethylene [Morschbacker, 2009]. There are varieties of Bio-PE such as linear low-density PE (LLPE), low density PE (LDPE) and high-density PE (HDPE), which depends upon method of polymerization used. In some instances, feedstock of bioethanol can also be extracted from sugarcane [Shen, Worrel, and Patel, 2010], cassava [Garcia et al., 2009], sugar beets [Belboom and Léonard, 2016] and potatoes [Farajpour et al., 2020]. Bioplastics are obtained from three major components including lipids, carbohydrates (starch, lignin, cellulose, chitosan) and proteins. The commonly researched bioplastics are proteins (plants and animals), cellulose esters, starch, polyhydroxyalkanoates (PHA) and polylactic acid (PLA) [Mekonnen et al., 2013]. In a research, algae was reported to be used as filler in different plastics and blended in different proportions with polyethylene (PE) [Otsuki et al., 2004], polyvinyl chloride (PVC) [Zhang et al., 2000]. Poly-β-hydroxybutyrate (PHB) is a bioplastic, biologically derived from cyanobacteria [Haase, Huchzermeyer, and Rath, 2012] and can also be used to produce biodegradable high-grade plastics [Balaji, Gopi, and Muthuvelan, 2013]. Chlamydomonas reinhardtii [Chaogang et al., 2010] and Phaeodactylum tricornutum were reported to produce PHB. Hydrodictyon reticulum [Balou, Babak, and Priye, 2020] was used to transform lactic acid (by fermentation and polymerization) to polylactic acid (PLA) which is a renewable biodegradable plastic.

The European Union regulation enlists novel foods which are safe for human consumption under Regulation (EU) 231 2015/2283. It comprises of microalgae as well such as Haematococcus pluvialis (green microalgae), Tetraselmis chuii (red algae) (European Commision 2017). Studies have shown that the intake of microalgae for human food and animal feed has increased in the recent times [Madeira et al., 2017]. The addition of microalgae to human consumption reported certain benefits such as anti-microbial properties [Pina-Pérez et al., 2017]. Animal feed added with microalgae (Schizochytrium sp and Chlorella sp.) has shown improved growth and better meat quality in animals like pigs, rabbits, poultry and ruminants. Chlorella vulgaris when supplemented to the feedstock of Capra aegagrus hircus, feed intake and digestibility has shown an improvement by 10-15% [Kholif et al., 2017]. An increase in PUFA (Polyunsaturated Fatty Acids) content has reported rise in milk yield. The presence of high quantity of carbohydrates and proteins in LEB than raw biomass makes it a good food supplement [Fan et al., 2020]. In aquaculture, fishes are dependent on microalgae as food containing PUFA thus helping fishes be useful source of oil for human consumption. In case of LEB, presence of adequate amount of lipids can make them a good candidate of feedstock in aquaculture [Khan, Shin, and Kim, 2018].

Saturated and mono-unsaturated fatty acids are the major contributor of algal biodiesel, while poly-unsaturated fatty acids are found to be not responsible for it [Kaur et al., 2012]. The PUFA however are reported to be beneficial for human consumption. It was reported by research that PUFA are helpful in averting CVDs (cardiovascular diseases), cancer [Caporgno and Mathys, 2018] and inflammatory diseases [Adarme-Vega, Thomas-Hall, and Schenk, 2014]. It consists of omega-3 fatty acids (eicosapentaenoic acid (EPA), docosahexaenoic acid (DHA) and omega-6 fatty acids (linoleic acid/LA, arachidonic acid/AA). These are extracted from fish at large scale as fish oil, used as supplements [Hixson et al., 2015]. The PUFA obtained from LEB would present new avenues of its utilization as vegetarian alternative for fish oil [Ryckebosch et al., 2014] [Chauton et al., 2015]. The LEB contains not only proteins and lipids but also carbohydrates in its composition. It was investigated that microalga derived carbohydrates have encouraging impacts on human health. Researchers have examined the anti-inflammatory effects of polysaccharides from Porphyridium sp. on skin [Levy-Ontman et al., 2017]. The extracts like monosaccharides (mannitol, glucose) from Isochrysis galbana and Nannochloropsis oculate has reported anticancer and antimicrobial characteristics [Hafsa et al., 2017]. During the step of cell disruption in lipid extraction, polysaccharides in the cell wall and extracellular matrix is released as byproduct. Such carbohydrate mixture consists of monosaccharides, comprising glucose, xylose, galactose [Bernaerts et al., 2018]. While the use and effect of these carbohydrates requires more studies focusing upon LEB co-products.

Bioethanol, a carbon-neutral fuel, is generally derived from plants and its waste materials [Hanaki and Portugal-Pereira, 2018]. The production of bioethanol takes place in anaerobic fermentation conditions using microbes like bacteria, fungi and yeast which can also be applied on LEB [Nguyen and Vu, 2012] [Robak and Balcerek, 2018]. The presence of carbohydrates such as glucose, mannitol, alginate, cellulose in algal biomass makes them suitable candidate for bioethanol production [Ale and Meyer, 2013]. In case of Chlorococcum sp., LEB was used to produce bioethanol, which yields 60-70% more ethanol than in original biomass [Kumar, Ghosh, and Pal, 2020]. In the processing of bioethanol, CO2 emanated can be used for microalgae cultivation which makes LEB a potential feedstock for it.

It was researched in Nannochloropsis sp. that when lipids and pigments are extracted, it serve as an appropriate source of biohydrogen [Nobre et al., 2013]. The presence of efficient amount of biohydrogen would also provide avenues to produce sufficient biogas amount. It requires combined production of hydrogen and methane which has reported to increase methane yield by 22% than from anaerobic digestion when performed using LEB of Scenedesmus [Yang et al., 2011]. Biogas is mixture of methane (55-75%) and CO2 (25-45%) produced through anaerobic digestion. In biorefineries, solid-state fermentation technologies are taken into consideration for the production of biogas as byproduct [Manan and Webb, 2017]. In Nannochloropsis sp., Tetraselmis sp. and Scenedesmus sp., the biogas production was reported to be higher when obtained from LEB in comparison to intact algal biomass [Milledge et al., 2019]. The increase in yield varies with species and may decrease also while it is also dependent upon cell disruption/pre-treatment methods used [Srinivasan and Kulshreshtha, 2020]. It implies that to make biogas and biohydrogen a chief by-product of LEB in biorefinery, processes such as harvesting and pre-treatment need to be considered for effective yield.

Biochar (BC) is a carbon compound produced by the pyrolysis of biomass [Sun et al., 2019]. The production of biochar from wet algal biomass takes less time under moderate temperature conditions [Gollakota, Kishore, and Gu, 2018]. The algal BC was reported to have low carbon content, compact surface area with high capacity of cation exchange in comparison to lignocellulosic-based BC [Michalak et al., 2019]. The high pH of algal BC makes it suitable for acidic soil, while high nitrogen content in BC would help soil maintain its fertility. The presence of different functional groups would allow algal BC as a potential option for remediation of wastewater [Awad et al., 2017] [Filote et al., 2019]. Algal biochar has been reported to have higher yield (8.1–62.4%) for per unit algal biomass compared to other feedstock such as plant waste, wheat straw [Ronsse et al., 2012] [Yu et al., 2017]. The variation in composition of different biomass (cell wall composition) reported decline in yield when pyrolysis conditions varied from 300-750°C, for change in time from 10-60 minutes [Tag et al., 2016]. The LEB obtained from Chlorella sp. utilized to produce BC has reported an efficient application in pyrolysis of residual biomass to produce bio-oil [Li et al., 2019]. A research report that shows pyrolysis process has enabled the formation of olive-shaped BC, which are magnetic in nature and can be catalyzed by iron [Salimi et al., 2019]. These magnetic BC have enhanced carbon structure with high surface area (296.4 m2 g−1) making them suitable as electrode for lithium-ion batteries. These are investigated to have enhanced cyclic stability compared to synthesized magnetic electrode with improved specific discharge capacity (740 mAh g−1) [De Bhowmick, Sarmah, and Sen, 2019] [Shukla et al., 2017].

The production of lipids for biofuel simultaneously with such materials presents wide applications making this approach potential for more research and investigation to implement it as economically more viable.

Pollutants possess adverse impacts on biotic and abiotic strata of the ecosystem either directly or indirectly [Renita, Kumar, and Jabasingh, 2019]. The extensive use of dyes and heavy metals since industrialization and urbanization has imposed a huge burden of environmental and economic losses on us [’t Lam et al., 2018] [Valentín-Reyes et al., 2019]. In a recent study, LEB was used for biosorption of methylene blue, acid fuchsin and acid blue 161 dyes [Maurya et al., 2014] [Sarat Chandra et al., 2015] [Da Fontoura et al., 2017]. The LEB of Fucus spiralis was used for the adsorption of lead [Filote et al., 2019]. Bio-sorbents based on LEB are investigated as potential byproduct of LEB for the adsorption of dyes and heavy metals when used without any modification and generates fewer secondary pollutants [Yu et al., 2017] [Amin, Chetpattananondh, and Ratanawilai, 2019]. Their disposal after remediation costs less environmental impacts and can be transformed into biochar [Wang et al., 2013]. This approach of conversion into biochar would provide more functional groups associated binding sites responsible for effective mitigation of wastewater. Hydrogels are water saturated 3-D structured crosslinked polymers having high adsorption capacities, reusable in nature and helpful for the treatment of wastewater [Mohammed et al., 2016]. While hydrogels dried at room temperature are named as xerogels. These xerogels have high porosity, very small size of pores and large surface area [Nayak and Das, 2018]. Hydrogels and xerogels are another such LEB based algal products valuable in various applications. Alginate obtained from Cystoseira trinodis (brown seaweed) when used for the synthesis of xerogels, it was reported to have enhanced porosity, density and other characteristics.

The idea to use the byproducts after lipid extraction reflects towards sustainable biorefinery approach [Gerardo et al., 2015] [Chew et al., 2017] [Fan et al., 2020]. However, it requires careful consideration of different processes [Gutiérrez, Serna, and Alzate, 2017] used during lipid extraction along with feedstock selection [Olguín, 2012], so as to produce valuable products to a maximum extent. Here LEB is utilized to obtain non-biofuel lipids and remaining value-added products. Carbon materials prepared from glucose has shown enhanced nitrogen retention [Chang, Tsai, and Li, 2015]. N-doped activated carbon made up of algae were reported to be useful for CO2 sequestration in gaseous waters [Khoshbouy, Takahashi, and Yoshikawa, 2019] [Matsagar et al., 2021]. In bio-composite fabrication incorporated with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), the use of LEB as a filler has reported enhanced thermal stability of PVA. However, it reduced tensile strength which can be compensated by using polydiallyldimethyl ammonium chloride (PD) as plasticizer [Yuan et al., 2016]. A research reported Microbial fuel cells (MFCs) using chlorella vulgaris at cathode and LEB as anode [Fang et al., 2018]. Here, LEB used as electron donor reduced the requirement of an external electron donor and thus also reduced the total cost. LEB use as anode material for the development of lithium-ion batteries and supercapacitors also possess scope for application of LEB [Ren et al., 2018] [Kalderis et al., 2014]. The introduction of nanotechnologies and other mild processes for lipid extraction and LEB based products helped to optimize cost and vitalize biorefinery as sustainable field of research [Nizami and Rehan, 2018] [Vanthoor-Koopmans et al., 2013]. The co-production of biogas with biohydrogen as discussed above presents one such biorefinery approach applicable on LEB. The downstream processing for a single product costs about 30% of final cost of production, while it raises up to 60% in case of multiple products [Lam et al., 2018]. There are various strategies developed till now for simultaneous production of valuable compounds from LEB [Yew et al., 2019]. Therefore, the integration of lipid extraction with LEB based products provides a sustainable future for the establishment of biorefineries.

The production of biofuels from first to fourth generation used different feedstocks such as edible and non-edible plants, algae and metabolic engineering in photosynthetic organisms. In the extraction of lipids, biomass (wet or dry) and other procedural operations requires careful consideration as the techniques also vary with the end product (algal compounds) targeted. The production of biodiesel at large scale serves as challenge in the development of this technology of biofuel. Some of the causes includes high consumption of energy, non-environment friendly and high capital cost during downstream processing. To enhance the yield of lipid from wet algal biomass, hydrothermal liquefaction (HTL) is one such process potentially effective for wet biomass. This eliminates drying and involves direct conversion of wet biomass into biocrude at high temperature of 200-400 °C and pressure of 6-15 MPa. The dominance of organic solvents in extraction indicates for new avenues of research in the development of green solvents. Such evolving green solvents include natural deep eutectic solvents, fluorous solvents and supramolecular solvents. These solvents have potency to substitute organic solvents and ionic liquids while their applicability is yet to explore more.

The green technique involved use of biodiesel as an extractant and later used to incubate wet biomass. The use of mechanical stress with methanol as a solvent mixture in the technique yielded 68% efficiency. This technique saves energy and enables recovery of solvents but implementation at large scale makes it costly. There are several parameters such as optimized extraction time and temperature, application of green solvents at large scale and direct transesterification of wet algal biomass for large scale production of biofuels, which requires further research to make it viable option for future. While the integration of different methods of cell disruption with traditional solvent-based methods of lipid recovery has to be delved deeper, in order to reduce the use of solvents as their recovery cost makes it extravagant. The sustainable use of algal biomass requires extraction of lipid along with production of value-added products from LEB. The extraction of different byproducts such as feedstock for animals, biohydrogen, biosorbents, biochar etc. makes the biorefinery a suitable approach. While factors like selection of algae, composition and conversion efficiencies, environment impacts, input energy cost and cost-benefit ratio at various stages of development leave it open for further investigation. However algal biomass grown in wastewater would not only serve as medium for culture but also contribute to remediation and CO2 sequestration. In various studies, unit operations are discussed while in some LEB based products and other possible products are mentioned. This review presents a holistic information of both so as to make algal biotechnology a thought-provoking field of research. This study contributes towards wider research on algal technology for economic, climate friendly and energy efficient methods of lipid extraction and sustainable solutions for biofuel production.
None.
The study did not involve any animal or human subject.
The authors give their consent to participate in this research work.
The authors give their consent to publish this original article.
Lakhan Kumar: Conceptualization, Writing- Original draft preparation
Raksha Anand: Writing-Review and editing draft.
Maulin P Shah: Conceptualization, Writing- Reviewing and Editing
Navneeta Bharadvaja: Conceptualization, Writing- Reviewing and Editing
